coredata.dc:description: Fire-risk assessment of lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) is an urgent task as the number and size of products using LIBs are both increasing. LIB electrolyte solvents consist of mixtures of flammable carbonate esters, such as ethylene carbonate (EC), dimethyl carbonate (DMC), ethyl methyl carbonate (EMC), and diethyl carbonate (DEC). This study aims to report the first EC combustion experiments (ignition delay time (IDT), CO laser absorption, and laminar flame speed measurements), and to provide a LIB electrolyte surrogate model that covers EC, DMC, EMC, and DEC pyrolysis and oxidation. The IDT and CO measurements were performed in a shock tube for EC/O2 mixtures, diluted with 95% Ar and 99.7% He/Ar, respectively, at equivalence ratios (ϕ) of 0.5, 1.0, and 2.0, a temperature range of 1228–1717 K, and at near-atmospheric pressures. Laminar flame speeds were measured in a closed vessel for H2/air mixtures seeded with 0.5% EC, at an initial temperature of 423 K and at atmospheric pressure. The LIB electrolyte surrogate model was constructed by implementing the EC oxidation sub-mechanism to our EC pyrolysis model with updates in the base mechanism. Experimental IDTs show little equivalence ratio dependence and are reasonably reproduced using the model proposed in this study. Measured CO time-history profiles show strong temperature dependence and a slight equivalence ratio effect. The model shows excellent agreement with the measured CO profiles at intermediate-to-high temperatures for ϕ = 0.5 and 1.0, while it underpredicts CO formation at low temperatures and at ϕ = 2.0. Based on numerical analyses, EC mostly undergoes decomposition to acetaldehyde, e.g., 90% at ϕ = 1.0 and 1499 K at CO onset, which drives the CO formation in our conditions. Oxidation as well as pyrolysis data of EC are still scarce, thus experimental studies with a wide range of methods, conditions, and properties are necessary for further model validation.

Definition method of the ignition delay time.

Ignition delay times of EC at ϕ = 0.5, 1.0, and 2.0 and near-atmospheric pressure in 95%Ar dilution. Error bars are shown only for the extreme conditions.
Experimental CO time-history profiles for EC oxidation at (a) ϕ = 0.5, (b) ϕ = 1.0, and (c) ϕ = 2.0 under dilution conditions and at near-atmospheric pressure and varied temperatures (1284–1717 K).

Experimental (grey) and computational (black) CO time-history profiles for EC oxidation at ϕ = 0.5 (top), 1.0 (middle), and 2.0 (bottom) at near-atmospheric pressures and representative temperatures: low (left), intermediate (middle), and high (right) at around 1300 K, 1500 K, and 1700 K, respectively.

Reaction pathways analysis for EC oxidation (0.086%EC/0.214%O2/20%He/79.7%Ar (ϕ = 1.0), 1499 K, and 1.04 atm) and pyrolysis (0.1%EC/20%He/79.9%Ar, 1484 K, and 1.02 atm) at 30% EC consumption (4–5 μs). The pyrolysis condition is from the experiment by Grégoire et al. [26]. Numbers in the parenthesis are the percent contributions of each reaction to the consumption of the parent species at oxidation and pyrolysis conditions. Arrows show the order of magnitude of the total rate-of-consumption for each reaction pathway (mol/cm3/sec).
CO sensitivity coefficients for (a) ϕ = 0.5, (b) ϕ = 1.0, and (c) ϕ = 2.0 at low, intermediate, and high temperatures. The coefficients were taken from the peak sensitivity value.
Laminar flame speed for H2/air mixtures neat and seeded with 0.5% mol. EC at 423 Κ as initial conditions.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Fire-risk assessment of lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) is an urgent task as the number and size of products using LIBs are both increasing. LIB electrolyte solvents consist of mixtures of flammable carbonate esters, such as ethylene carbonate (EC), dimethyl carbonate (DMC), ethyl methyl carbonate (EMC), and diethyl carbonate (DEC). This study aims to report the first EC combustion experiments (ignition delay time (IDT), CO laser absorption, and laminar flame speed measurements), and to provide a LIB electrolyte surrogate model that covers EC, DMC, EMC, and DEC pyrolysis and oxidation. The IDT and CO measurements were performed in a shock tube for EC/O2 mixtures, diluted with 95% Ar and 99.7% He/Ar, respectively, at equivalence ratios (ϕ) of 0.5, 1.0, and 2.0, a temperature range of 1228–1717 K, and at near-atmospheric pressures. Laminar flame speeds were measured in a closed vessel for H2/air mixtures seeded with 0.5% EC, at an initial temperature of 423 K and at atmospheric pressure. The LIB electrolyte surrogate model was constructed by implementing the EC oxidation sub-mechanism to our EC pyrolysis model with updates in the base mechanism. Experimental IDTs show little equivalence ratio dependence and are reasonably reproduced using the model proposed in this study. Measured CO time-history profiles show strong temperature dependence and a slight equivalence ratio effect. The model shows excellent agreement with the measured CO profiles at intermediate-to-high temperatures for ϕ = 0.5 and 1.0, while it underpredicts CO formation at low temperatures and at ϕ = 2.0. Based on numerical analyses, EC mostly undergoes decomposition to acetaldehyde, e.g., 90% at ϕ = 1.0 and 1499 K at CO onset, which drives the CO formation in our conditions. Oxidation as well as pyrolysis data of EC are still scarce, thus experimental studies with a wide range of methods, conditions, and properties are necessary for further model validation.

ce:section.ce:para: Lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) are embedded in many products in our daily life, such as mobile phones and laptops, owing to their high energy density, portable size, and lack of memory effect. With the greater global interest in renewable energy, the demand for larger-scale LIBs is growing in the energy storage systems and transportation industry [1]. A main concern of the use of LIBs is, however, fire incidents. Since a LIB is composed of flammable electrolytes, thermal runaway triggered by mechanical, electrical, and thermal abuses will lead to fire and, potentially, explosion under certain circumstances. LIBs are equipped with a venting system to release inner pressure and avoid a rupture of the cell, which ejects electrolyte gases and aerosols as well as partially reacted gas species during or prior to the thermal runaway event [2,3]. The vent gas composition is largely diverse depending on various factors in such a scenario (e.g., electrolyte composition, electrode material, and state-of-charge of a battery), making it difficult to evaluate a fire risk. In addition, the cathode can decompose and release oxygen as it suffers from the increasing temperature at around 200–300 °C [4–6].

Understanding these series of LIB fire mechanisms is crucial from the fire safety aspect, especially for the implementation of the larger-scale applications. Zhang et al. [7] demonstrated a thermal runaway simulation of a LIB to identify its safe-limit by varying heat source temperature and duration assumed as internal short circuit induction parameters, but without considering chemical reactions. Cellier et al. [8] performed a large eddy simulation for a forced ignition of vented gases from a LIB cell using a reduced chemical kinetic model, assuming that the electrolyte components are not in the vent gases in vapor phase but are already decomposed into smaller flammable molecules (H2, CO, CO2, and C1–C3 hydrocarbons, see Mathieu et al. [9] and reference therein for the composition of the flammable vent gas). Motivated by the LIB fire risk under the situation where the vaporized electrolytes and partially reacted gases exist, the present study focuses on gas-phase chemistry of electrolyte solvents. A comprehensive gas-phase chemical kinetic model of LIB electrolytes is necessary not only to enhance these simulations but also to understand the role of the vaporized electrolytes during LIB fires.

Together with lithium salts, linear and cyclic carbonate esters, such as dimethyl carbonate (DMC), ethyl methyl carbonate (EMC), diethyl carbonate (DEC), and ethylene carbonate (EC), as shown in Fig. 1 , are widely used as electrolyte components [4,10]. Typical compositions of commercially available lithium hexafluorophosphate (LiPF6)-based LIB electrolytes are listed in Table 1 . EC is an important component in the electrolyte composition from an electrochemical view point, as it facilitates the dissociation of lithium salts thanks to its large dielectric constant [4]. On the other hand, due to its high viscosity and the fact that EC is in solid phase at standard temperature and pressure, co-solvents with a low viscosity such as the linear carbonate esters are needed. The global combustion properties, i.e., ignition delay time and laminar flame speed, of DMC [11–16] and DEC [17–19] as well as detailed chemical kinetic models of the linear carbonate esters [17,20–23] have been studied by several groups. To the best of the authors' knowledge, however, those types of data have not been reported for EC. Similarly, speciation data and a detailed chemical kinetic model for the EC oxidation are yet to be available. In our previous work [24], speciation data for EC/DMC pyrolysis were obtained using a micro flow reactor with a controlled temperature profile (MFR) [25], and the first EC pyrolysis sub-mechanism was constructed based on theoretical calculations. More recently, CO laser absorption measurements for neat EC pyrolysis were successfully performed using a shock tube at near-atmospheric pressure, and updates in the chemical kinetic model of EC were proposed [26]. The CO time-history measurement has great advantages to validate a model and to gain kinetics insight into the CO formation, especially for oxygenated fuels as there would be two different CO formation channels, i.e., pyrolysis-derived CO formation and oxidation-derived CO formation. The importance of oxygenate chemistry during the pyrolysis of linear carbonate esters (DMC, EMC, and DEC) has also been discussed in a previous study using shock-tube CO laser absorption measurements [27]. In the present study, CO time-history profiles as well as ignition delay times for the oxidation of EC were measured for the first time in a shock tube. Laminar flame speeds were also measured over a wide range of equivalence ratios for a H2/air mixture seeded with 0.5% EC.

The goal of this study was to obtain the first experimental EC oxidation characteristics using a shock tube and a closed combustion vessel, and to propose a detailed chemical kinetic model for EC that also includes the linear carbonate esters, i.e., DMC, EMC, and DEC, hereafter called as a LIB electrolyte surrogate model. The ignition delay time and CO laser absorption measurements were conducted for EC oxidation with diluted mixtures. The laminar flame speed measurements were performed for H2/air mixtures seeded with EC. The LIB electrolyte surrogate model was validated with the experimental data and numerical analyses were performed, especially to investigate the CO formation during EC oxidation. The subsequent sections describe this work in detail.

The oxidation of EC in diluted mixtures was carried out for three equivalence ratios (ϕ = 0.5, 1.0, and 2.0) in a shock-tube. EC is in a solid state at standard temperature and pressure. To allow the study of this component in the gas phase, a custom-made heating jacket was used to maintain the shock tube at a uniform temperature of between 100 °C (CO measurements in 99.7% He/Ar) and 120 °C (ignition delay time (IDT) measurements in 95% Ar) to ensure that the EC was maintained in the vapor phase during the experiments. A custom-made heating jacket is used for the mixing tank as well, and its temperature was set to 120 °C (CO) or 140 °C (IDT). Additionally, heating elements and fiberglass insulation set at 120 °C (CO) or 140 °C (IDT) were also applied on the critical experimental components, such as the manifold and the vial containing the solid compound.

To prevent any possible condensation, especially during the mixture preparation, as much as 0.19 kPa of EC was used in the mixture preparation for CO measurements and 0.96 kPa for the IDT measurements, to obtain the fuel-rich mixture (ϕ = 2.0). The heating system ensures that the pressure of EC remained constant and well-below the recorded maximum of 1.5 kPa at 140 °C (note that this does not constitute an accurate measurement of the saturating vapor pressure of EC). The mixtures were prepared as follows: the coarsely powdered EC was introduced in a heated vial and was then degassed multiple times to ensure no unwanted air was introduced into the mixture. The vaporized EC was transferred and isolated into the mixture tank via the heated manifold, and the absence of condensation in the manifold was verified by vacuuming it until no pressure was detected and confirming no increase in pressure was observed when the vacuum system was turned off. The pressure of EC introduced in the tank was validated by re-opening the tank to the manifold after the vacuuming phase, before continuing with the next gases, namely O2, (He,) and Ar additions. Note that due to the difficulty in vaporizing EC, the final mixtures were large enough for a couple of experiments only for IDT measurements. The results presented herein are thus the results of many mixture preparations. The solid EC comes from Sigma Aldrich with a purity of 98%, and the gases, O2, He, Ar, and air (21% O2 in N2) were provided by Praxair, all with 99.999% purity.

The study of the chemical kinetics for EC with ignition delay time and CO laser absorption measurements at high temperatures and near atmospheric pressure was performed using a shock tube located at Texas A&M University. This facility consists of a driver section (7.62-cm inner diameter and 3.25-m long) and a driven section (16.2-cm inner diameter and 7.88-m long) separated by a single polycarbonate (0.25-mm thickness) diaphragm. Helium is filled in the driver section until the diaphragm breakage and relaxes towards the low-pressure section, forming a shock wave propagating into the test gas. At the end of the tube, the reflected shock generates an ideal experiment with constant temperature and pressure for about 2 ms. Five PCB P113A22 piezoelectric pressure transducers are placed on the sidewall along the end of the driven section, which allow the measurement of the incident shock wave velocity by detecting the passage of the shock wave. As the last pressure transducer is located very close to the endwall (1.6 cm), the incident velocity can be inferred at the endwall by extrapolation [28]. The post-reflected-shock conditions were calculated using the 1-D normal shock equations (T5 and P5) obtained with the initial temperature and pressure conditions, T1, P1, the speed of the shock wave, vs , and the initial mole fractions of the mixtures. The final T5 and P5 can be determined within ±0.8% and ±1.0%, respectively [29].

As previously mentioned, a vacuum system, including a vane pump and a turbomolecular pump, enables a pressure of 10−5 kPa prior to each experiment to maintain high purity for these experiments. More details on the shock tube can be found in Mathieu et al. [30]. Finally, the experimental results were obtained for temperatures ranging from 1228 to 1717 K and pressures ranging from 0.98 to 1.69 atm for three equivalence ratios ϕ = 0.5, 1.0, and 2.0. Table 2 reports the experimental conditions covered during this study.

Ignition delay times were measured both at the sidewall location (same location as the CO diagnostic below) and at the endwall of the shock tube. In both cases, pressure and OH* chemiluminescence were measured. At both locations, the OH* chemiluminescence was measured using 307-nm filters with a 10-nm full width at half maximum. At the sidewall location, a 1-mm slit was used along with a concave focusing mirror redirecting the OH* beam path to a Hamamatsu photomultiplier tube in a custom-made housing. A similar photomultiplier was used at the endwall location. Despite the relatively high dilution level used (95% Ar), the experiments exhibited a noticeable increase in pressure at the ignition event. The IDT was thus measured at the endwall location and is defined as the time interval between the detection of the incident shock wave at the endwall location and the time at which the tangent to the maximum slope of the OH* signal crosses the baseline, as visible in Fig. 2 . The overall uncertainty on these IDT measurements is estimated at around 25%, which includes the uncertainties on T5 and P5 discussed above, the uncertainty in the measurements, and the uncertainty in the mixture due to the very low vapor pressure of EC.

The CO laser absorption measurements are performed at the end of the shock tube using the two sapphire optical window ports. A tunable quantum cascade laser produces coherent light near 4.8 μm to monitor the P(20) line of the
1 ← 0
band of CO and is centered at 2059.91 cm−1 prior to each test with a removable cell containing a low pressure mixture of CO in 90% Ar. This quantification technique works in relative isolation from H2O and CO2 absorption [32] as well as other intermediates found during EC combustion such as CH4, C2H2, C2H4, C2H6, and CH3CHO [26]. The laser beam is divided into two intensities, namely the incident and transmitted intensities, I 0 and I t, where I 0 represents the neat signal without interaction with CO, and I t is the transmitted signal that goes through the reacting gases in the shock tube and gets absorbed by CO, decreasing its intensity. These two intensities are collected by two InSb detectors after passing optical elements (irises, lenses, and bypass filters) and are processed to obtain time histories of CO mole fractions, based on the Beer-Lambert relation, defined as

This calibration was obtained using a mixture of 2000 ppm CO in 0.2 He/0.798 Ar, replicating similar dilution and collision partners (He and Ar) at atmospheric pressure and a goodness-of-fit R2 value of 0.9984 was determined.

During the experiments, the exothermicity from the decomposition of the EC induces an increase in the temperature T5 (
Δ
T
= 25 and 40 K at most for the highest temperatures of ϕ = 0.5 and 2.0, respectively). The absorption coefficient can be computed as a time-varying parameter in Eq. 2 by implementing simulated time-varying temperatures with the current kinetics model. Finally, previous studies estimated the uncertainties of the CO measurements at around 5.5% [26] and a representative CO time-history measurement for the oxidation of EC in 0.997 He/Ar at ϕ = 1.0 is presented in Fig. 3 (see Fig. S1–3 in Supplementary Material 1 for the other conditions and all the experimental data are available in Supplementary Material 2).

All laminar flame speed experiments were conducted using the High-Temperature-High-Pressure (HTHP) constant volume vessel at Texas A&M University. The experimental setup consists of a vessel specifically designed to study flame propagation and combustion processes under controlled conditions. The vessel is a cylindrical chamber with a total volume of approximately 25.8 liters. The vessel has a sturdy construction to withstand the high temperatures and pressures involved in the experiments. Its internal diameter measures 31.8 cm, and it has a length of 28 cm. To maintain the desired experimental conditions, the vessel is equipped with a heating jacket. This jacket has the capability to heat the vessel up to 600 K. Furthermore, the vessel is designed to handle high-initial-pressure tests with pressures up to 10 atm. To facilitate optical diagnostics, the vessel is fitted with two opposed optical accesses. These window ports provide different viewing angles and optical paths to observe and analyze the flame propagation. Each optical access is equipped with a high-quality glass window measuring 6.4 cm in thickness. These windows are designed to withstand the high temperatures and pressures within the vessel while providing a clear optical pathway for optical diagnostics. More details about this apparatus can be found here [34–37]. A modified z-type schlieren imaging setup was used to capture the spherically propagating flame. The schlieren setup consists of a light source generated by a mercury lamp and sent through the vessel, then captured by a high-speed Photron FastCam SA 1.1 camera. The high-speed camera has the capability of capturing up to 25,000 frames/second. The captured images are then processed using an in-house Python code for edge detection. The non-linear equation developed by Chen [38] is then used to get the burning velocity. Chemical kinetics modeling is then used to get the burned-to-unburned density ratio needed to obtain the unstretched, unburned flame speed.

The tested mixtures were prepared using the partial pressure method. However, some difficulties were encountered since, as reported before, EC is in a solid state (i.e. crystal) at standard conditions, making it difficult to vaporize and very prone to condense on cold spots. This fact also resulted in an uncertainty in the reported laminar flame speed to be higher than usual. The usual uncertainty in the laminar flame speed obtained using the current flame speed apparatus is 3% [39], while the reported laminar flame speed in the current study has a 10 – 20 % uncertainty. Neat EC flame speed measurements were not attainable due to the physical properties of EC, making it hard to evaporate enough EC in the HTHP vessel to the desired partial pressure. Therefore, in the current study, EC was used as an additive to hydrogen/air mixtures with a fixed percentage of 0.5% vol. for all conditions. A special procedure was adopted to perform these experiments. The adopted procedure was to transfer the EC from a stainless-steel vial, whereas the hydrogen (H2) and air were added using a manifold connecting the different gas cylinders and the HTHP vessel. The stainless-steel vial containing the EC was hooked up to a heated-manifold next to the HTHP vessel. The vial containing the EC was wrapped with heating tapes and heated up to 100 °C. The heated manifold was flushed twice with EC vapors to remove any other substance. Then, the EC was introduced to the HTHP vessel to the desired pressure equivalent to the targeted 0.5% in volume percentage. Following this series of steps, H2 and air were then added to their desired pressures to satisfy the targeted equivalence ratio. When all components were introduced to the HTHP vessel, the mixture was left to rest for 10 minutes to ensure that the mixture was well-mixed.

An EC oxidation model was constructed upon our previous EC pyrolysis model [24], which also includes sub-mechanisms of the linear carbonate esters (DMC, EMC, and DEC) [23]. The newly constructed model — LIB electrolyte surrogate model — would be the first comprehensive chemical kinetic model that covers widely used LIB electrolyte components, both the linear and cyclic carbonate esters, and thus, enables us to predict gas-phase reactions of multi-component LIB electrolytes. In addition to the construction of an EC oxidation sub-mechanism, updates in the base mechanism and the linear carbonate esters sub-mechanisms were also made as described in the following paragraphs. Key reactions in the present EC sub-mechanism are summarized in Table 3 . The present LIB electrolyte surrogate model consists of 160 species and 961 reactions and is available together with the thermochemical and transport data in chemkin format in Supplementary Material 3–5.

In the present EC sub-mechanism, two initiation reactions of EC were added (with the reaction number corresponding to number of the recation in the model): EC ⇆ CY(CJOC*OOC) + H ( − R945) and EC + O2 ⇆ CY(CJC*OOC) + HO2 (R946). The reaction rate constants were analogically taken from those of 1,3-dioxolane, which is a five-member ring with two O-atoms included (1,3-dioxolane has a structure similar to EC but with a >CH2 group instead of a >C=O for the carbon atom between the two oxygen atoms in the ring), studied by Wildenberg et al. [40,41]. Based on their recent theoretical calculations for H-atom abstruction reactions from 1,3-dioxolane [41], the H-atom abstruction reactions from EC by OH, H, CH3, and HO2 were updated accordingly. Theoretical calculations for the decomposition of the EC radical, CY(CJOC*OOC), indicated that the major decompostion channel is the dissociation into CH2CHO and CO2 (R957), and the ring opening β-scission reaction producing O*CCOCJ*O (R958) is the second energetically favored channel [24]. The subsequent reactions to O*CCOCJ*O, O*CCOCJ*O ⇆ CH2CHO + CO2 (R959) and O*CCOCJ*O ⇆ OCH2CHO + CO (R960), were determined based on the analogy to one of the ring-opened product from 1,3-dioxolane (O*CJOCC). The rate constants of R957–R960 were taken from [24,41]. Molecular structures of the EC radical and ring-opened isomer are listed in Table S1 in Supplementary Material 1.

The base mechanism was updated in the present model according to the recent shock-tube studies on the pyrolysis of DMC, EMC, DEC, and EC by Grégoire et al. [26,27]. AramcoMech 1.3 [42] was replaced with the C0–C2 base mechanism from NUIGMech 1.1 [43,44] in the present model. NUIGMech 1.1 well-reproduced experimentally obtained the global combustion properties, e.g., ignition delay time and laminar flame speed, for H2/CO/CO2/C1–C3 hydrocarbons mixed gases [9], which are reported to be released during the LIB thermal runaway events [1]. In EMC and DEC pyrolysis, methanol and ethanol chemistry, respectively, were found to have a potential impact on the time-dependent CO prediction [27]. Similarly, acetaldehyde and ethylene oxide (oxirane, C2H4O1–2) chemistry showed high sensitivities and rate-of-production contributions to the CO profile during EC pyryolysis [26]. Modifications made for reactions of ethanol, acetaldehyde, C2H4O1–2, and C2H3O1–2 [26,27] were applied to the present model.

The DMC, EMC, and DEC sub-mechanisms were also updated in the present model. Some reactions related to DMC, EMC, and DEC were reviewed by Grégoire et al. [27] based on CO time-history measurements during pyrolysis. As was suggested in our previous studies based on CO2 measurements for DMC oxidation/pyolysis [24,45], a theoretically obtained rate constant for DMC ⇆ CH3OCH3 + CO2 by Zhang et al. [46] at 1 atm was fitted to the 2-parameter Arrhenius expression and used in the present study. See Fig. S4–7 in Supplementary Material 1 for the validation results of the present model with laminar flame speeds and ignition delay times of DMC and DEC from the literature.

Experimental characteristics of EC oxidation (ignition delay times and CO time-history profiles) and the validation of the present model are shown in Section 4.1 and 4.2. Then, detailed chemical kinetic analyses on the CO production are performed using the model proposed in the present study. Finally, laminar flame speeds of H2/air mixtures seeded with EC are presented in Section 4.3 to validate the model. All the experimental data are available in the Supplementary Material 2.

Experimental and computational ignition delay times (IDTs) of EC at ϕ = 0.5, 1.0, and 2.0 in 95% Ar and at near-atmospheric pressure in the shock tube are shown in Fig. 4 . Experimental results show only a minor effect of equivalence ratio on IDT. By comparing ϕ = 0.5 and ϕ = 2.0, the ignition delay time of the fuel-lean case tend to be longer than the fuel-rich case. The effective activation energies of IDT in both fuel-lean and fuel-rich cases appear to be quite similar, and they are greater than the activation energy observed for the stoichiometric case. Consequently, this leads to shorter IDT values on the low-temperature side and longer IDT values on the high-temperature side when compared to the cases with ϕ = 0.5 and 2.0, respectively. Computational results are in reasonable agreement with the measured IDTs considering the relatively larger uncertainty in the experiments than usual due to the difficulty in the preparation of EC mixtures. The present model slightly overpredicts the IDT at ϕ = 0.5, especially for the high-temperature side. On the other hand, it underpredicts the IDT at ϕ = 2.0 for the low-temperature side. The effective activation energy of IDT in the computational result at ϕ = 1.0 is larger than that in the experimental result, leading to the underprediction and overprediction at the low- and high-temperature sides, respectively.

Measured CO mole fractions as a function of time during EC oxidation at ϕ = 0.5, 1.0, and 2.0, near-atmospheric pressure, and varied temperatures under 99.7% He/Ar dilution in the shock tube are shown in Fig. 5 . Note that the two spikes that appear in the CO profile at around 0 μs are due to the rapid density change in the mixture caused by the passage of incident and reflected shock waves across the laser beam path, which results in the temporary beam steering, so they are not due to CO formation. At the fuel-lean (ϕ = 0.5, Fig. 5a) and stoichiometric (ϕ = 1.0, Fig. 5b) conditions, the time-dependent CO mole fractions display a two-stage CO formation. First the CO mole fraction gradually increases, and this first formation stage is fully visible at low-temperature conditions, e.g., 1284 K for ϕ = 0.5, within the measured time frame. The second increase in the CO mole fraction appears at intermediate-temperature conditions, e.g., 1384 K for ϕ = 0.5, reaching a peak CO mole fraction of approximately 1000 ppm and 1800 ppm at ϕ = 0.5 and 1.0, respectively. The position of the peak shifts to shorter delay-times as temperature increases, and at high-temperature conditions, e.g., 1695 K for ϕ = 0.5, the CO profile shows one continuous rapid increase followed by a monotonically decrease (the decrease being due to the CO-to-CO2 oxidation). The CO mole fractions reach their peak both at around 250 μs for the highest temperature cases at ϕ = 0.5 (1695 K) and 1.0 (1717 K), while the peak values are different, around 1000 ppm and 1600 ppm, respectively. Similarly, at the fuel-rich conditions (ϕ = 2.0, Fig. 5c), the CO profile shows the gradual increase at low-temperature conditions (1303 K); the two-stage increase at intermediate-temperature conditions (1499 K); and the rapid, continuous increase at the high-temperature condition (1704 K). The shape after reaching a maximum value of CO mole fraction, however, differs from the fuel-lean and stoichiometric cases, showing almost a plateau, which is similar to the pyrolysis cases [26]. Moreover, CO mole fraction for the highest temperature case at ϕ = 2.0 (1704 K) becomes a maximum of approximately 3500 ppm at around 500 μs, which is two times slower than those at ϕ = 0.5 and 1.0 for a similar temperature of 1706 ± 11 K. The conversions of EC to CO at the maximum-CO time are 67%, 62%, 90%, and 125% at ϕ = 0.5, 1.0, 2.0, and pyrolysis, respectively, with the pyrolysis results from our previous study [24].

Fig. 6 shows the comparison between the measured and computed CO time-history profiles for EC oxidation at representative low-, intermediate-, and high-temperature conditions. See Fig. S8 in Supplementary Material 1 for all temperature cases. The present LIB electrolyte surrogate model satisfactorily reproduces the measured CO mole fractions. The model shows excellent agreement with the intermediate- and high-temperature results at the fuel-lean and stoichiometric conditions, while it underpredicts CO yields at low-temperature and/or fuel-rich conditions. The similar trend to the latter was also reported by Grégoire et al. [26] for EC pyrolysis in the shock tube using our previous EC pyrolysis model. They made an update on reactions mainly related to C2H4O1–2, and their tentative model showed improvements in the prediction of CO time-history profiles, especially in the timing of the CO peaks at intermediate temperatures. Their tentative model, however, also continued to underpredict the CO mole fraction, which led to the conclusion that a deeper look into acetaldehyde chemistry by validating models with such CO laser-absorption measurements would be necessary as acetaldehyde was a main pyrolysis product from EC. In this context, they conducted CO time-history measurements for acetaldehyde pyrolysis and found that their model as well as literature models overestimated CO mole fraction, which was the opposite trend to the EC pyrolysis [26]. Although the trend was different, reviewing the acetaldehyde chemistry would be the first step to filling the experimental and computational discrepancy. This conclusion would be similar to the low-temperature and/or fuel-rich conditions in oxidation as well if the dominant products were the stable acetaldehyde and CO2 from EC thermal decomposition. The conversion of EC to CO at the peak-CO value is 64% for the computational result at ϕ = 2.0 and 1704 K, being almost the same as those at ϕ = 0.5 and 1.0. This outcome would also suggest that insights into the detailed kinetics for C2 species at fuel-rich conditions is important, a conclusion that was also reached in a recent study with DEC oxidation under similar conditions [19].

Reaction pathways for EC oxidation at ϕ = 1.0, near-atmospheric pressure, 1500 K, and where 30% of EC is consumed (4–5 μs) are depicted in Fig. 7 , together with a pyrolysis case as a comparison. Computational results using the present model for the EC pyrolysis conditions [26] are shown in Fig. S8 in Supplementary Material 1. The major pathways of EC oxidation and pyrolysis are similar under the conditions considered, i.e., the onset of CO formation. The dominant EC consumption pathway is the unimolecular decomposition reaction producing acetaldehyde and CO2, EC ⇆ CH3CHO + CO2 (R942), which accounts for 90% of EC consumption, and the second dominant pathway produces ethylene oxide and CO2, EC ⇆ C2H4O1–2 + CO2 (R943), contributing 8% to the EC consumption. Since C2H4O1–2 is unstable as compared to CH3CHO [47], the subsequent reactions for C2H4O1–2 rapidly produces HCO (via C2H4O1–2 ⇆ CH3 + HCO (R264)) and then CO (via HCO + M ⇆ H + CO + M (R187)). Consequently, this reaction pathway (EC → C2H4O1–2 → HCO → CO) drives the CO formation at the beginning until subsequent reactions for CH3CHO become significant at around 30 μs. It should be noted that an isomerization of C2H4O1–2 to CH3CHO has accounted for about half of the C2H4O1–2 consumption under EC/DMC pyrolysis at the maximum wall temperature of 1200 K in MFR using the previous EC pyrolysis model [24], but this isomerization has a minor contribution to C2H4O1–2 consumption with the present LIB electrolyte surrogate model. This difference results from the updates in the C2H4O1–2 decomposition reactions. The rate constants of C2H4O1–2 ⇆ CH3 + HCO (R264) and C2H4O1–2 ⇆ CH3CHO (R265) are similar in AramcoMech 1.3 and NUIGMech 1.1, whereas those theoretically obtained by Joshi et al. [47] indicate a difference of two orders of magnitude at 1200 K where R264 is dominant. The rate constants and branching ratio of R264 and R265 are shown in Fig. S9 in Supplementary Material 1 (although there are seven decomposition pathways reported in the literature [47], only two of them that are included in AramcoMech 1.3 and NUIGMech 1.1). The H-atom abstraction reactions from EC show a small contribution (ca. 2%) mostly by EC + H ⇆ CY(CJOC*OOC) + H2 (R948). Both two subsequent reactions to the EC radical produce CH2CHO, followed by the decomposition into CO directly or via CH2CO. The latter pathway competes with HCCO formation where a slight difference is observed between the oxidation and pyrolysis conditions as it produces CO and CO2 at oxidation but produces solely CO at pyrolysis.

The second increase in the CO time-history profile is driven by mainly two pathways, CH2O → HCO → CO and HCCO → CO (see Fig. S10 in Supplementary Material 1 for the CO rate-of-production (ROP) analysis at the representative temperatures for the oxidations). Based on the ROP analysis, CH3 + O ⇆ CH2O + H (R115), CH2 + O2 ⇆ CH2O + O (R83), and C2H4 + O ⇆ CH2 + CH2O (R300) and C2H2 + O ⇆ HCCO + H (R378) were the major CH2O and HCCO production reactions at around 700–800 μs, respectively. CO oxidation to CO2 is enhanced at the later stage (around 1000 μs) where the net CO rate of production turns negative, and the experimental and computational CO mole fractions start decreasing. The change in the major CO formation pathways at ϕ = 1.0 as time proceeds is illustrated in Fig. S11 in Supplementary Material 1.

Together with the ROP analysis, the CO sensitivity analysis was performed to investigate the equivalence ratio and temperature effects on the CO formation. The top 10 sensitive reactions at ϕ = 0.5, 1.0, and 2.0 and at around 1300, 1500, and 1700 K are shown in Fig. 8 . A general trend is discussed first, followed by temperature/equivalence ratio dependence trends. R943 (EC ⇆ C2H4O1–2 + CO2) is the most sensitive reaction for CO formation at all conditions. The onset of the CO formation is especially sensitive to this reaction (see Fig. S12 in Supplementary Material 1 for the sensitivity coefficient as a function of time) as the reaction pathway via C2H4O1–2 (EC → C2H4O1–2 → HCO → CO) proceeds faster than that via CH3CHO. In contrast, R942 (EC ⇆ CH3CHO + CO2) shows a negative sensitivity coefficient at all conditions as it competes with R943. C2H4O1–2 mostly produces HCO (R264: C2H4O1–2 ⇆ CH3 + HCO), followed by the HCO decomposition to CO (R187: HCO + M ⇆ H + CO + M), producing H-radical in the net reaction system. Although CH3CHO also proceeds toward CO via HCO (R423: CH3CHO (+M) ⇆ CH3 + HCO (+M)) or CH3CO (R428: CH3CHO + H ⇆ CH3CO + H2), the latter consumes H-radical. Thus, R264 and R423 exhibit a positive sensitivity coefficient while R428 shows a negative sensitivity coefficient to the CO formation in general. Note that although R428 possesses the positive sensitivity coefficient at its peak sensitive value at high-temperature/fuel-rich conditions in Fig. 8, it seems to have a negative sensitivity overall to the CO formation over the test time as apparent in Fig. S12. Finally, as HCO is one of the major sources of CO, its decomposition reaction R187 shows a positive sensitivity at all conditions considered. In contrast, the CO formation is less sensitive to the decomposition reaction of CH3CO, CH3CO (+M) ⇆ CH3 + CO (+M) (R446).

Comparing the low versus intermediate and high temperatures, the CO formation is relatively sensitive to R948 (EC + H ⇆ CY(CJOC*OOC) + H2), − R945 (EC ⇆ CY(CJOC*OOC) + H), R470 (CH2CHO ⇆ CH3 + CO), and R267 (C2H4O1–2 ⇆ CH3CO + H) at low temperatures for at least two equivalence ratio cases. At fuel-rich condition, R948 shows a positive sensitivity at the intermediate temperature as well, and R434 (CH3CHO + CH3 ⇆ CH3CO + CH4) is listed only at ϕ = 2.0. To understand the influence of H-atom abstraction reactions from EC on the CO formation, we attempted a trial computation using the rate constants of the H-atom abstraction reactions with the analogy to tetrahydrofuran [48] (e.g., the rate constant theoretically obtained for the H-atom abstraction with OH-radical from tetrahydrofuran at C-atom adjacent to O- and another C-atom [49] is approximately four times higher than that of 1,3-dioxalane at the similar configuration [41] at 1000 K and 1 atm), but this change in the rates coefficients did not modify the computed CO mole fractions. It is also worth mentioning that the rate constants of the H-atom abstraction with H-radical from 1,3-dioxalane and tetrahydrofuran at the aforementioned position hardly differ, the latter one [50] is 1.4 times faster than the former one [41]. As for R945, the same rate constant as 1,3-dioxalane is used in THF [48,50] based on the analogy to alkane [51]. Although a careful attention is needed for how far we can apply the analogy to oxygenates, these comparisons between the two similar molecular structures that involve O-atom(s) in their five-member ring suggest that the H-atom abstractions from EC seem not be a primary factor for the CO underprediction. Meanwhile, reactions involving oxygenated intermediates such as aldehydes and alcohols play important roles in the CO time-history profile in particular for carbonate esters [19,26,27] and need to be further investigated at fuel-rich/pyrolysis conditions and low-to-intermediate temperatures. Under these conditions, model predictions for the time-history profiles of CO and H2O vary among well-known mechanisms and show a relatively large discrepancy from experiments even for C0–C3 blended gaseous or ethanol/CO2 mixtures [9,52]. Modification in the C0–C2 chemistry essentially affects not only the CO profile but also whole combustion and pyrolysis properties, which is a huge project and beyond the scope of the present work.

As preparing the neat EC/air mixture is challenging due to the physical properties of EC, laminar flame speed measurements were carried out for H2/air mixtures seeded with 0.5% EC at 423 K and 1 atm. The main purpose of this measurement is to validate the (EC sub-)model with laminar flame speeds of EC-containing mixtures for which well-investigated H2/air mixtures were selected as a base component. The combustion chemistry of hydrogen is well-known (see Fig. S13 in Supplementary Material 1 for a comparison between laminar flame speed data [36,37] and model predictions for H2/air mixtures at various temperatures), and it is assumed that the combustion chemistry of EC can be tested this way, since a large difference in the laminar flame speed results is observed compared to the neat H2 mixture, which reflects the combustion chemistry of EC. The flame speeds of H2 and EC mixtures are also important for LIB fires since the thermal runaway gases potentially involve both components. Despite a small addition of EC, 0.5% mol., a large effect can be seen on the laminar flame speed, Fig. 9 . According to the model predictions, the maximum flame speed is reduced by about 13%, and the equivalence ratio at which this maximum flame speed is reached is shifted: 1.7 for the neat H2/air mixture, and 1.55 for the mixture seeded with EC. The model is in good agreement with the data for equivalence ratios of 1.2 and lower, and between ϕ = 2.0 and 2.5. The maximum flame speed at the peak is however over-estimated by about 15%, and the model seems to diverge from the data above ϕ = 2.5. Overall, given the uncertainty in the data due to the difficulties associated with the study of EC in gas phase, it can be concluded that the model can satisfactorily predict the data. For further validation and updates of the model, combustion and pyrolysis experiments of EC with a wide range of methods, conditions, and properties are required.

A lithium-ion battery (LIB) electrolyte surrogate model, consisting of a comprehensive detailed chemical kinetic model for the major LIB electrolyte components (dimethyl carbonate (DMC), diethyl carbonate (DEC), ethyl methyl carbonate (EMC), and ethylene carbonate (EC)), is proposed, with this study focusing on the EC sub-mechanism. Ignition delay time (IDT) and CO laser absorption measurements for EC oxidation (ϕ = 0.5, 1.0, and 2.0) in diluents were conducted over the temperature range of 1228–1717 K and at near-atmospheric pressures behind reflected shock waves in a shock tube. The experimental IDTs showed a small effect of the equivalence ratio, and the present LIB electrolyte surrogate model reasonably reproduced the experimental results. The measured CO time-history profiles showed a strong temperature dependence, with a gradual and linear increase in the CO profile at low temperatures, a two-step increase at intermediate temperatures, and a continuous and rapid increase at high temperatures. These trends were well-predicted using the present model although the computational results underpredicted the CO mole fractions at low-temperature and/or fuel-rich conditions. CO sensitivity analysis showed a high sensitivity for reactions related to C2 oxygenates, e.g., acetaldehyde and ethylene oxide, suggesting the need for updates in the oxygenate chemistry. Laminar flame speeds of H2/air mixtures seeded with 0.5% EC were measured at 423 K and 1 atm for ϕ = 0.8–3.0 using a closed combustion vessel and were well-reproduced with the present model. Although additional experimental data with an extended range of combustion characteristics and experimental approaches are still required for the validation and improvement of the model, the present LIB electrolyte surrogate model will be useful to predict combustion properties of the electrolyte components and assess the fire risks of LIBs. Eq. (1)

Due to the growing use of lithium-ion batteries, their flammability risk needs to be assessed. The flammability of LIBs is linked to the use of carbonate esters for the electrolyte. Among these electrolyte components, ethylene carbonate has been the topic of a very limited number of studies, focusing only on its pyrolysis chemistry. The present study investigates the oxidation of EC for the first time using a wide variety of experiments. A new detailed kinetics model was developed and validated against these experiments.
